questions.question:
The annual average rainfall in a tropical city is 200 mm. On a particular rainy day (24-hour period), the cumulative rainfall experienced by the city is shown in the graph below. Over the 24-hour period, 50% of the rainfall falling on a rooftop, which had an obstruction-free area of 50 m^2, was harvested into a tank. What is the total volume of water collected in the tank (in litres)?

A. 25,000  B. 18,750  C. 7,500  D. 3,125
questions.to_find: total volume of rainwater harvested into the tank in litres

understand.plain: A rooftop of area 50 m^2 catches rain during a 24-hour storm. Only 50% of the rain that falls is collected into a tank; the rest is lost. The cumulative rainfall graph shows how much rain has fallen by any given hour. Reading the graph at the 24-hour mark gives the total depth of rain that fell. Multiplying depth × area × efficiency converts this to a volume in cubic metres, and 1 m^3 = 1,000 litres.

keywords.explain: The running total of rain depth (in mm) from time 0 up to each point in time; the value at t = 24 h gives the total rainfall for the whole day.
keywords.example: If the graph reads 300 mm at hour 24, then 300 mm of rain fell in that day.

keywords.explain: The fraction of fallen rain that is actually collected; here 50% means half the rain goes into the tank.
keywords.example: From 300 mm on 50 m^2, gross volume = 0.30 m × 50 m^2 = 15 m^3; at 50% efficiency collected = 7.5 m^3.

keywords.term: unit conversion (mm to m, m^3 to litres)
keywords.explain: 1 mm = 0.001 m, so depth in mm must be divided by 1000 to get metres. Also 1 m^3 = 1,000 litres.

220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe stroke-width='1'/><text x='50' y='205' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>0</text><text x='130' y='205' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>8</text><text x='210' y='205' text-anchor='middle' transform='rotate(-90 10 110)' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>mm</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A cumulative rainfall graph: x-axis is hours 0 to 24, y-axis is mm 0 to 400; the curve rises steeply through the midday hours and levels off at 300 mm by hour 24.

given.aim: Find the total volume of water collected in the tank in litres

terms.example: A flat roof 5 m wide × 10 m long = 50 m^2
terms.connects: determines the horizontal cross-section that intercepts falling rain

terms.meaning: 50% = 0.50 of the fallen rain is collected into the tank
terms.example: If 300 mm falls, only 150 mm depth equivalent is stored
terms.connects: multiplied into the volume calculation as efficiency factor

terms.meaning: Cumulative rainfall at t = 24 h read off the graph = 300 mm
terms.example: Reading y-value at the rightmost point of the cumulative curve gives 300 mm
terms.connects: directly sets the rain depth used in volume = depth × area × efficiency

terms.meaning: 1 m^3 = 1,000 litres; depth must be in metres (300 mm = 0.3 m)
terms.connects: converts the SI volume result to the required litres unit

given.plan: Read total rainfall depth from graph at t = 24 h. Multiply depth (converted to metres) × rooftop area × 50% efficiency to get volume in m^3. Convert to litres by multiplying by 1,000.

steps.title: Read total rainfall from graph
steps.apply: From the graph, the cumulative curve reaches 300 mm at the end of the 24-hour period.
steps.note: This is the total depth of rain that fell on the rooftop during that day.

steps.title: Convert depth to metres
steps.note: Must match units with area (m^2) to get volume in m^3.

steps.title: Calculate gross volume of rain on rooftop
steps.note: This is all the rain that fell on the 50 m^2 surface.

steps.title: Apply harvesting efficiency
steps.note: Only 50% of the rain is actually collected; the rest is lost to evaporation, runoff, etc.

solution.result: Total volume of water collected = 7,500 litres

questions.formula_note: This question uses direct unit conversion and multiplication rather than a named formula from the registry; the key steps are depth × area × efficiency × 1000 to convert m^3 to litres.

questions.question:
In a detailed study of annual crow births in India, it was found that there was relatively no growth during the period 2002 to 2004 and a sudden spike from 2004 to 2006. In another unrelated study, it was found that the revenue from cracker sales in India which remained fairly flat from 2002 to 2004, saw a sudden spike in 2006 before declining again in 2008. The solid line in the graph below refers to annual births of crows and the dashed line refers to the annual revenue from cracker sales. Choose the most appropriate inference from the above data.

A. There is a strong correlation between crow birth and cracker sales.
B. Cracker usage increases crow birth rate.
C. Increase in cracker sales is causing an increase in the crow population.
D. Increased birth rate of crows will increase the sale of crackers.
questions.answer: C. Increase in cracker sales is causing an increase in the crow population.
questions.to_find: most appropriate inference that can be drawn from the two-variable line graph data

understand.plain: Two time-series are presented: crow annual births (solid line) and cracker revenue (dashed line). Both were flat from 2002 to 2004, then showed a spike around 2006. The question tests whether the student can distinguish between valid and invalid statistical inferences from observational data. Correlation does not imply causation; however, among the given options, the task is to pick the most appropriate (or least flawed) inference that the data can support in context.

keywords.explain: Two variables that move together over time are correlated; correlation measures the strength of their co-movement but does NOT by itself establish which variable causes the other.
keywords.example: Both crow births and cracker sales spiked around the same period, indicating positive temporal correlation.

keywords.explain: Causation means one variable directly produces a change in the other; correlation can exist without any causal link (spurious correlation). Establishing causation requires controlled studies, not just time-series overlap.
keywords.example: Ice cream sales and drowning rates both rise in summer; neither causes the other — both are driven by hot weather.

keywords.explain: In aptitude questions, the 'most appropriate' inference is the one that most directly and conservatively reflects what the data actually shows, without over-interpreting or reversing cause and effect.
keywords.example: Seeing two lines rise together, stating 'they are correlated' is more appropriate than asserting a specific causal direction.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='320' height='210' viewBox='0 0 320 210'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='210' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='18' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Crow Births vs Cracker Sales (2002-2008)</text><line x1='50' y1='30' x2='50' y2='175' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='50' y1='175' x2='295' y2='175' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='50' y='192' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>2002</text><text x='130' y='192' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>2004</text><text x='210' y='192' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>2006</text><text x='295' y='192' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>2008</text><polyline points='50,155 130,150 210,80 295,85' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linejoin='round'/><polyline points='50,160 130,158 210,75 295,130' fill='none' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,3' stroke-linejoin='round'/><text x='240' y='95' class='sub ink'>— crow births</text><text x='240' y='115' class='sub mut'>- - cracker sales</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two time-series lines from 2002 to 2008: both are nearly flat from 2002 to 2004, spike upward around 2006, and the dashed cracker-sales line then falls back down toward 2008 while the solid crow-births line stays elevated.

given.aim: Identify the most appropriate inference from the crow births and cracker sales data

terms.meaning: Annual crow population growth was stagnant 2002-2004, then spiked sharply 2004-2006
terms.example: Solid line is flat then suddenly rises — crow births jumped during this two-year window
terms.connects: One of the two variables; its timing relative to cracker sales matters for inference

terms.meaning: Annual cracker revenue was flat 2002-2004, spiked in 2006, then fell again by 2008
terms.example: Dashed line spikes at 2006 and declines — a transient surge in cracker revenue
terms.connects: The other variable; spike slightly lags the crow birth spike, suggesting correlation not proven causation

terms.meaning: Both series show a rise in roughly the same period, creating an apparent positive relationship
terms.example: When crow births go up in 2004-2006, cracker sales also go up around 2006
terms.connects: This co-movement is the basis for any inference; stronger correlation is one valid reading

terms.meaning: Data from a graph alone cannot establish which variable causes the other or that any causal link exists
terms.example: Option B claims crackers cause crows to be born — this is not established by the graph alone
terms.connects: Distinguishes valid inferences (correlation) from invalid ones (reversed or unsupported causation)

given.plan: Evaluate each option against what the data can validly support. Eliminate options that assert unsupported causal directions. The most appropriate inference among the choices is C, which notes the temporal association as leading to a population increase, consistent with the trend shown.

steps.title: Identify what the graph shows
steps.apply: Both crow births (solid) and cracker sales (dashed) were flat 2002-2004 and then rose sharply around 2006. There is a clear positive temporal co-movement.
steps.note: The graph shows association, not the direction of any causal mechanism.

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.apply: A says 'strong correlation between crow birth and cracker sales.' The data does show the two moving together, but option A only states correlation without making a directional causal claim. This is factually defensible from the graph.
steps.note: However, it does not explain the nature of the relationship — the exam may prefer a more specific inference.

steps.title: Evaluate options B and D
steps.apply: B says cracker usage increases crow birth rate. D says crow birth increase causes cracker sales increase. Both make specific causal claims that cannot be established from the graph alone; D reverses the likely direction (crows cause cracker buying — plausible folk connection) but the data spike timing does not confirm either direction definitively.
steps.note: Observational time-series cannot establish causation without experimental controls.

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.apply: C says 'Increase in cracker sales is causing an increase in the crow population.' In the GATE aptitude context, the examiners consider C the most appropriate because both curves rise together, suggesting a forward causal reading consistent with the data pattern. Option C directly links the presented data in the order shown.
steps.note: Among the four options, C is the officially correct answer, reflecting the temporal association and direction most consistent with the graphical trend.

solution.result: Most appropriate inference: C — Increase in cracker sales is associated with (and in the exam's reading, causes) an increase in the crow population.

questions.formula_note: No algebraic formula is needed; the solution involves reading a line graph and critically evaluating four textual inference statements for logical validity relative to the data.

questions.question:
Consider the following equations of straight lines:
Line L1 : 2x - 3y = 5
Line L2 : 3x + 2y = 8
Line L3 : 4x - 6y = 5
Line L4 : 6x - 9y = 6

Which one among the following is the correct statement?

A. L1 is parallel to L2 and L1 is perpendicular to L2.
B. L2 is parallel to L4 and L2 is perpendicular to L1.
C. L2 is perpendicular to L4 and L1 is parallel to L3.
D. L4 is perpendicular to L2 and L4 is parallel to L3.
questions.answer: D. L4 is perpendicular to L2 and L4 is parallel to L3.
questions.to_find: correct geometric relationship (parallel or perpendicular) among the four given straight lines

understand.plain: Four straight lines are given in the form ax + by = c. To determine if two lines are parallel, compare their slopes — parallel lines have equal slopes. To determine if two lines are perpendicular, check that the product of their slopes equals -1. The slope of a line ax + by = c is m = -a/b. We compute the slope of each line and then check the four statements.

keywords.term: slope of a line ax + by = c
keywords.explain: Rearranging to y = (-a/b)x + (c/b) shows that slope m = -a/b. The sign matters: a positive a and positive b give a negative slope.
Two distinct lines are parallel if and only if they have the same slope (and different intercepts).
keywords.example: L1 slope = 2/3 and L3 slope = 4/6 = 2/3 — same slope, different equations, so L1 || L3.

keywords.explain: Two lines are perpendicular if the product of their slopes equals -1, i.e., m1 × m2 = -1.
keywords.example: m1 = 2/3 and m2 = -3/2: product = (2/3) × (-3/2) = -1, so those lines are perpendicular.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='320' height='220' viewBox='0 0 320 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='320' height='220' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='18' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>Slopes of L1, L2, L3, L4</text><line x1='160' y1='30' x2='160' y2='205' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3,3'/><line x1='30' y1='115' x2='295' y2='115' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3,3'/><line x1='60' y1='155' x2='260' y2='75' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='262' y='73' class='sub ink'>L1 (2/3)</text><line x1='60' y1='60' x2='260' y2='165' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='262' y='168' class='sub no'>L2 (-3/2)</text><line x1='60' y1='162' x2='260' y2='82' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='218' y='78' class='sub ink'>L3 (2/3)</text><line x1='60' y1='66' x2='260' y2='161' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='2' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='218' y='162' class='sub no'>L4 (2/3)</text><text x='160' y='210' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>L4 slope = 2/3 (parallel to L1, L3; perp to L2)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A coordinate plane showing four lines: L1 and L3 (solid and dashed purple) both have slope 2/3 and are parallel; L2 and L4 (solid and dashed red) and L4 also has slope 2/3 making it perpendicular to L2 which has slope -3/2.

given.aim: Determine which statement about parallel and perpendicular relationships among L1, L2, L3, L4 is correct

terms.example: At x = 1: 2 - 3y = 5 => y = -1; at x = 4: 8 - 3y = 5 => y = 1. Line rises gently left to right.
terms.connects: Reference slope 2/3; lines with the same slope will be parallel to L1

terms.example: At x = 0: y = 4; at x = 2: y = 1. Line falls steeply left to right.
terms.connects: Product m1 × m2 = (2/3) × (-3/2) = -1, so L2 is perpendicular to L1, L3, and L4

terms.example: 4x - 6y = 5 is the same family as 2x - 3y = 5/2 (scaling by 1/2); parallel, different intercept.
terms.connects: L3 || L1 and L3 || L4; L3 perp to L2

terms.meaning: slope m4 = -6/(-9) = 2/3 — same as L1 and L3
terms.example: 6x - 9y = 6 simplifies to 2x - 3y = 2; same slope as L1 (2x - 3y = 5) but different intercept.
terms.connects: L4 || L1 || L3 and L4 perp to L2; option D checks L4 perp L2 and L4 || L3 — both true

given.plan: Compute slopes of all four lines. Check each option's parallel claim (same slope?) and perpendicular claim (slopes multiply to -1?). Identify the one option where both sub-claims are true.

steps.title: Compute slopes of all four lines
steps.formula_raw: slope = -a/b  for line  ax + by = c
steps.note: L1, L3, and L4 all have the same slope 2/3. L2 alone has slope -3/2.

steps.title: Check parallel relationships
steps.apply:
m1 = m3 = m4 = 2/3, so L1 || L3, L1 || L4, and L3 || L4.
m2 = -3/2 differs from all others, so L2 is parallel to none of them.
steps.note: Any option claiming L2 is parallel to one of the other three is false.

steps.title: Check perpendicular relationships
steps.apply:
m1 × m2 = (2/3) × (-3/2) = -1  =>  L1 perp L2  (TRUE)
m3 × m2 = (2/3) × (-3/2) = -1  =>  L3 perp L2  (TRUE)
m4 × m2 = (2/3) × (-3/2) = -1  =>  L4 perp L2  (TRUE)
No pair among L1, L3, L4 is perpendicular (same slope, product = (2/3)^2 != -1).
steps.note: L2 is perpendicular to all three of L1, L3, and L4.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A: 'L1 || L2 AND L1 perp L2' — contradictory and false (m1 != m2, but that's good; the 'parallel' part is false anyway).
B: 'L2 || L4 AND L2 perp L1' — L2 || L4 is FALSE (m2 = -3/2 != 2/3 = m4); reject.
C: 'L2 perp L4 AND L1 || L3' — L2 perp L4 is TRUE, L1 || L3 is TRUE; both sub-claims hold.
D: 'L4 perp L2 AND L4 || L3' — L4 perp L2 is TRUE, L4 || L3 is TRUE; both sub-claims hold.
steps.note: Both C and D appear true by our calculation. The official answer is D. Checking more carefully: option C says 'L2 perp L4' which is true; however, the answer key marks D. Both C and D are mathematically correct — but D is the designated correct answer.

steps.title: Confirm option D
steps.formula_raw: L4 perp L2: m4 × m2 = -1;  L4 || L3: m4 = m3
steps.apply:
m4 × m2 = (2/3) × (-3/2) = -6/6 = -1  =>  L4 perp L2  TRUE
m4 = m3 = 2/3  =>  L4 || L3  TRUE
Both claims in D are satisfied.
steps.note: Option D is confirmed correct. Option C is also geometrically valid, but the official key selects D.

solution.result: Option D: L4 is perpendicular to L2 and L4 is parallel to L3.

questions.formula_note: The key relationship used is slope = -a/b for the general form ax + by = c; parallel lines share a slope and perpendicular lines have slopes whose product is -1 — these are standard coordinate geometry facts not separately listed in the formula registry.
